title: MeshWatch: Service Mesh Observability at $5/Month
excerpt: A full observability platform (metrics, traces, logs, AI incident analysis) built on a single $5/month k3s node with Istio mTLS, achieving 60% cost reduction vs serverless alternatives while maintaining 99.8% uptime.
content:
TL;DR: MeshWatch delivers production-grade observability — metrics, distributed traces, log aggregation, and AI-assisted incident analysis — on a single k3s node costing $5.12/month, a 60% reduction over comparable serverless setups, at 99.8% uptime and a 45ms average response time.

The Problem

Managed observability stacks (Datadog, New Relic, AWS CloudWatch) scale linearly with ingest volume and host count. For a homelab running five microservices with Prometheus scraping at 15s intervals, the managed bill crosses $12.80/month before the first alert even fires. The core question: can a single-node cluster provide the three pillars of observability — metrics, logs, traces — plus an AI incident responder, without sacrificing reliability?

Architecture

The system runs on one k3s node (1 CPU, 2GB RAM) with Istio injected as a per-pod sidecar. Every service pod carries an Envoy proxy that enforces mTLS and emits telemetry. Data flows in three parallel pipelines:

  • Metrics: Envoy → Prometheus (15s scrape) → Grafana (20-panel dashboards)
  • Logs: Pod stdout → Promtail → Loki (7-day retention)
  • Traces: OpenTelemetry SDK → Istio egress → Tempo (span storage)
  • AI layer: Prometheus alert → Ollama Phi-3 via Tailscale tunnel → root-cause markdown

Istio's STRICT mTLS policy means zero plaintext traffic between any two pods. Certificates rotate automatically every 30 days through Istio's built-in CA.

Deep-Dive: Cost Engineering

The 60% savings come from three compounding decisions. First, k3s strips the Kubernetes control plane to ~512MB, leaving 1.5GB for workloads — enough for the entire observability stack. Second, Loki's chunk-compressed log format costs 1/10th the storage of Elasticsearch for the same ingest rate. Third, running Ollama Phi-3 locally eliminates per-query LLM API charges: at 50 alerts/day, that's $0 vs ~$30/month on OpenAI pricing.

Deep-Dive: AI Incident Analysis

When Prometheus fires an alert, the incident pipeline captures the last 5 minutes of affected metrics, queries Grafana for the relevant panels, and ships structured JSON to Ollama Phi-3 over an outbound-only Tailscale tunnel (100.65.214.138:11434). The model returns a natural-language root-cause analysis with remediation steps in ~2s. No metrics data ever leaves the mesh. This caught an Istio sidecar memory leak that raw Prometheus thresholds missed.

Deep-Dive: Canary Deployments

Flagger drives traffic splitting at the Istio gateway level: 10% → 25% → 50% → 100%, with Prometheus SLO checks at each stage. If the error rate exceeds 0.5% during canary, Flagger reverts traffic to stable within 30 seconds — no human in the loop.

Results

  • Monthly cost: $5.12 (60% below serverless parity)
  • Uptime: 99.8% across 15 pods and 5 monitored services
  • Response time: 45ms average, 0.2% error rate
  • Annual savings: $92.16 vs the serverless baseline

Lessons Learned

The biggest surprise was Istio's sidecar memory overhead — each Envoy proxy eats ~50MB, which on a 2GB node adds up fast. I capped the mesh to 15 pods before hitting memory pressure. A single-node cluster also means no control-plane HA; if the node reboots, there's a 90s observability blind spot. For production workloads I'd add a second node, but for a homelab the trade-off is acceptable. Finally, Phi-3's 1-thread quantization is fast but occasionally hallucinates remediation steps — every AI suggestion still needs a human sign-off before action.

title: Minecraft Monitoring: Full-Stack Observability with Discord Integration
excerpt: Bridging a PaperMC game server into a Kubernetes observability stack using JMX exporters, RCON protocol, and a Discord.py bot with 10 slash commands — achieving 99.8% uptime with automated TPS-drop alerting.
content:
TL;DR: A Minecraft server (PaperMC 26.1.2, Java 25) instrumented with JMX metrics, controlled via a Discord.py bot with 10 slash commands, and deployed on k3s with Istio — achieving 99.8% uptime and sub-second server control from a chat window.

The Problem

Minecraft server administration is traditionally a SSH-plus-screen workflow: type list in a console, read raw TPS, restart manually on crash. There's no metrics history, no alerting, and no way for a Discord community to self-serve server status. The goal was to treat a game server like any other production service — observable, alertable, and controllable from a familiar interface.

Architecture

The server runs as a StatefulSet on k3s with three integration layers:

  • JMX Exporter — exposes Java TPS, heap usage, GC pauses, and player count to Prometheus
  • RCON Protocol — TCP control channel for /restart, /whitelist, /ban, /tpo, /gamerules
  • Discord.py Bot — 10 slash commands that query Prometheus for read operations and RCON for writes

The bot runs as a sidecar in the same pod, so RCON calls hit localhost:25575 with zero network hops. Prometheus queries go over the service mesh with mTLS enforced by Istio.

Deep-Dive: The 10 Slash Commands

The command surface splits cleanly into read and write paths. Read commands (/status, /players, /tps, /tps-graph) query Prometheus and render inline Discord embeds — no server load. Write commands (/restart, /whitelist, /ban, /tpo, /gamerules) open an RCON session, execute, and close within 200ms. /help is static. Every write command requires a Discord role check before the RCON socket opens.

Deep-Dive: Automated Alerting

Prometheus alert rules fire on two thresholds: TPS below 18 (lag) and heap usage above 400MB (memory pressure). When either trips, a webhook posts a Discord embed with the last 5 minutes of metrics — no dashboard needed. The alert pipeline deduplicates within a 5-minute window to prevent storming during a sustained degradation.

Deep-Dive: GC Pause Tuning

Java 25's ZGC collector kept the last GC pause at 45ms, well under the 100ms threshold where Minecraft players notice rubberbanding. The JMX exporter surfaces GC pause percentiles to Grafana, making it possible to correlate player complaints with garbage collection events.

Results

  • Uptime: 99.8% over a 30-day window
  • Mean RCON command latency: ~180ms (including Discord round-trip)
  • Last GC pause: 45ms (ZGC, Java 25)
  • Alert dedup window: 5 minutes, zero alert storms recorded

Lessons Learned

RCON has no built-in auth beyond a shared password — I put it behind Istio authorization policies so only the bot's service account can reach port 25575. The JMX exporter adds ~30MB of heap overhead; on a 2GB node that's noticeable, so I tuned the scrape interval to 30s rather than 15s for game-server metrics. Finally, Discord slash commands require bot registration and a 1-hour global cache for command metadata — plan deployments around that window.

title: GitOps Monitoring Platform: ArgoCD, cert-manager, and External Secrets
excerpt: A production-grade monitoring platform driven by ArgoCD App-of-Apps, with External Secrets Operator syncing Azure Key Vault, cert-manager TLS auto-provisioning, and a 20-panel Grafana dashboard — all declaratively managed in Git.
content:
TL;DR: A monitoring stack where every component — Prometheus, Loki, Grafana dashboards, TLS certs, secrets — is declared in Git and reconciled by ArgoCD, with zero manual kubectl apply in production. Seven alert rules, 20 Grafana panels, 5 services monitored, 7-day log retention.

The Problem

Manual Kubernetes deployments drift. A kubectl apply here, a helm upgrade there, and within a week nobody knows what's actually running. The goal: a monitoring platform where Git is the single source of truth, every change is reviewed, and the cluster self-heals to the declared state.

Architecture

ArgoCD sits at the top, watching a Git repo structured with the App of Apps pattern: a root Application points to a directory of child Applications, each deploying one component into its own namespace.

  • kube-prometheus-stack (v85.2.1 Helm chart) — Prometheus, Alertmanager, Grafana
  • Loki + Promtail — log aggregation, 7-day retention
  • External Secrets Operator — syncs Azure Key Vault → Kubernetes Secrets
  • cert-manager — Let's Encrypt TLS for all ingress routes

Every namespace has its own Application CRD, so a misconfigured Loki deploy can't block a Prometheus rolling update.

Deep-Dive: External Secrets Operator

Storing Grafana admin passwords and Discord webhook URLs in Git is a non-starter. External Secrets Operator bridges this: a ExternalSecret CRD references an Azure Key Vault object, and the operator syncs it into a native Kubernetes Secret on a 1-hour refresh. The Secret in-cluster is the same object Prometheus reads — no app code changes. If Key Vault rotates the secret, the operator updates the Secret, and Grafana picks it up on the next pod restart.

Deep-Dive: cert-manager TLS Auto-Provisioning

Every ingress route terminates TLS with a Let's Encrypt certificate provisioned by cert-manager. An Issuer CRD per namespace handles the ACME challenge via HTTP-01. Certificates auto-renew 30 days before expiry. The win: zero manual cert operations across 5 ingress routes, and expired certs — the #1 cause of cascade failures in my earlier homelab — are structurally impossible.

Deep-Dive: The 20-Panel Grafana Dashboard

The Minecraft monitoring dashboard has 20 panels organized into four rows: cluster health (CPU, memory, pod count), JVM internals (heap, GC rate, thread count), game metrics (TPS, player count, chunk load time), and Istio mesh (request rate, mTLS status, circuit-breaker trips). Dashboard JSON lives in a ConfigMap that ArgoCD reconciles — so a dashboard edit is a Git commit, not a click in the Grafana UI.

Results

  • Services monitored: 5 across 4 namespaces
  • Alert rules: 7 (TPS, heap, pod restarts, cert expiry, mesh errors, log volume, node pressure)
  • Grafana panels: 20 on the primary dashboard
  • Log retention: 7 days, ~1.2GB indexed
  • Manual production applies: 0 — everything is GitOps

Lessons Learned

App of Apps is powerful but the root Application is a single point of failure — if ArgoCD can't reach the repo, nothing reconciles. I keep a local ArgoCD backup and document the manual recovery path. External Secrets Operator's 1-hour sync can mean a stale secret for up to 60 minutes; for critical rotations I trigger a manual refresh. cert-manager's Let's Encrypt rate limits (50 certs/domain/week) bit me once during a testing binge — now I use the staging issuer for iteration.

title: Serverless Incident Response with Azure Functions and Service Bus
excerpt: A Python serverless layer that processes incident events from Azure Service Bus, deduplicates within 5-minute windows, and routes Discord webhook embeds — decoupling alert generation from notification delivery.
content:
TL;DR: Two Azure Functions (Python v2.0 runtime) — a 15-minute health checker and a Service Bus-triggered incident processor — that decouple alert generation from notification delivery, with Pydantic-validated messages and 5-minute deduplication windows to prevent alert storms.

The Problem

When Prometheus fires an alert, something has to turn that event into a human-readable Discord notification. Coupling notification logic directly into the Prometheus Alertmanager template means every notification change is a configmap edit and an ArgoCD sync. The goal: a decoupled serverless layer that consumes incident events from a queue, validates them, and routes them to Discord — independently deployable and independently scalable.

Architecture

Two functions, two triggers:

  • Health Checker — Timer Trigger, 15-minute cron. Polls each service's health endpoint, emits an incident event to Service Bus on failure.
  • Incident Processor — Service Bus Trigger on the incident-events topic. Validates the message, deduplicates, and posts a Discord embed webhook.

Service Bus is the decoupling boundary: Prometheus Alertmanager and the health checker both produce events; the incident processor is the sole consumer. Adding a new notification channel (Slack, PagerDuty) means a new consumer, not a change to the producers.

Deep-Dive: Pydantic Message Validation

Every Service Bus message is validated against a Pydantic model before processing. The model enforces required fields (service name, severity, timestamp, metric value) and rejects malformed payloads with a dead-letter queue entry rather than a silent drop. This caught a producer bug where Alertmanager sent severity as "WARNING" instead of "warning" — the validator normalized it before it reached Discord.

Deep-Dive: 5-Minute Deduplication

A sustained degradation can fire the same alert every 30 seconds. The incident processor tracks a 5-minute dedup window keyed on (service, alert-rule): if an event arrives within the window, it's acknowledged to Service Bus but not forwarded to Discord. This dropped alert noise by ~80% during a 2-hour Istio sidecar memory leak.

Deep-Dive: Async HTTP with aiohttp

The Discord webhook POST runs on aiohttp rather than requests, so the function doesn't block on the webhook round-trip. A failed webhook delivery retries three times with exponential backoff, then dead-letters the message for manual inspection.

Results

  • Functions deployed: 2 (health checker + incident processor)
  • Health check interval: 15 minutes, 96 checks/day per service
  • Dedup window: 5 minutes, ~80% noise reduction during incidents
  • Webhook delivery: 3 retries with exponential backoff, dead-letter on exhaustion

Lessons Learned

Azure Functions cold start adds 1–3s to the first invocation after idle — acceptable for a 15-minute health checker, painful for a latency-sensitive alert path. I keep the incident processor warm by sending a heartbeat event every 5 minutes. Service Bus pricing is per-operation, so the dedup window isn't just a UX win — it cuts costs by dropping ~80% of duplicate receives. Finally, Pydantic v2's validation is fast but the migration from v1 broke some of my field aliases; pin your Pydantic version in requirements.txt.

title: Building a Terminal-Themed PWA Portfolio with Zero Dependencies
excerpt: A synthwave-aesthetic portfolio built with vanilla JS, zero build step, and 115 passing tests — featuring a 28-command terminal, command palette, 10 unlockable achievements, and WCAG 2.1-compliant accessibility deployed on Cloudflare Pages for $0.
content:
TL;DR: A portfolio site with a working terminal emulator, 28 commands, command palette (Ctrl+K), 10 unlockable achievements, PWA offline support, and WCAG 2.1 accessibility — built with zero npm dependencies in the shipped bundle, zero build step, 115 passing tests, and hosted on Cloudflare Pages for $0/month.

The Problem

Most portfolio sites are either a static template (forgettable) or a React/Next.js app (heavy, 200KB+ JS, needs a build pipeline). The goal: a portfolio that demonstrates engineering skill by being the demonstration — an interactive terminal that's fast, accessible, offline-capable, and trivially deployable, with no framework tax.

Architecture

No build step. Files ship as-is: HTML pages import ES modules from /js/, CSS is a single stylesheet with custom properties for theming. The module graph:

  • terminal.js — main controller, 28 commands, command palette, demo mode, achievements
  • project-catalog.js — centralized project metadata; command count derived from COMMAND_ICONS keys
  • meshwatch-api.js — GitHub OAuth PKCE + Azure Functions proxy for live metrics
  • ai-assistant.js — Ollama Phi-3 client with cached knowledge fallback
  • service-worker.js — PWA offline cache, fetch-first strategy, versioned (v6)
  • utils/helpers.js — escapeHtml, normalizeSlug, validateUrl, perf thresholds

Every user-facing string passes through escapeHtml() before any innerHTML assignment — XSS hardening is a convention, not an afterthought.

Deep-Dive: The Terminal Emulator

The terminal isn't a toy — it's a state machine with command history (up/down arrow), tab autocomplete against the command registry, and a Ctrl+K command palette that fuzzy-matches. The 28 commands span navigation (help, whoami, projects), interactive (explorer, dashboard, writeups), and utility (theme, achievements, perf). Command count is derived dynamically from Object.keys(COMMAND_ICONS).length — adding a command means adding an icon, and the count updates everywhere automatically.

Deep-Dive: Accessibility and PWA

WCAG 2.1 compliance: skip links, ARIA roles on every interactive element, keyboard navigation for the terminal and all cards, prefers-reduced-motion respected by every animation. The PWA service worker (v6) uses a fetch-first strategy with an offline fallback page, caching 31536000s for static assets and 600s for config. The manifest declares icons up to 512px for installability.

Deep-Dive: Testing Without a Framework

Tests use Node.js's native node --test runner — no mocha, no jest. 115 tests across 14 files mock DOM via JSDOM-like assertions and run in pure Node. The CI gate is npm test in GitHub Actions, which blocks the Cloudflare Pages deploy on any failure. ESLint enforces single quotes, semicolons, 2-space indent; terminal.js is the one eslintignored file due to a known ESLint v8 parse bug with its import+comment patterns.

Deep-Dive: Zero-Cost Deployment

GitHub Actions (pages.yml) runs npm test, then cloudflare/pages-action@v1 deploys the root directory. Cloudflare handles SSL, CDN, and custom domain (chai-homelab.com) on the free tier. The only paid infrastructure is Azure Blob Storage ($0.50/month) for the API gateway. Total portfolio hosting: $0, unlimited bandwidth.

Results

  • Bundle size: 0 npm dependencies in shipped output
  • Tests: 115 passing, 0 failures, native Node runner
  • Commands: 28, derived dynamically from the icon registry
  • Lighthouse: 95+ across performance, accessibility, and PWA
  • Monthly hosting cost: $0 (Cloudflare Pages free tier)

Lessons Learned

Zero build step is liberating but means no tree-shaking — every module must be hand-curated for size. terminal.js self-instantiates at the module bottom (new Terminal()), which means test files that import it get a live instance; I use before() hooks to manage state. The service worker cache name (career-portal-v6) must be manually bumped on any asset change — I've forgotten twice, and the stale-cache bug is subtle. Finally, vanilla JS at this complexity requires discipline: the escapeHtml()-before-innerHTML convention is enforced by code review, not a framework.
